prompt.name: setup
prompt.content:
Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or attempt to do them out of order.

Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by analyzing the codebase,
configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing example stories for 9 components.

Work through these steps in order. After each story file, run Vitest to verify it renders.
If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it passes before moving on.

### Step 1: Analyze the codebase

Before writing any stories, understand what the components need to render:

- Scan the project for context providers, theme systems, routers, stores, and i18n setups.
  Look at the app's entry point (e.g. `App.tsx`, `main.tsx`, `layout.tsx`) to see what
  providers wrap the component tree.
- Identify global CSS or style imports required for components to look correct.
- Note any path aliases configured in tsconfig or bundler config.
- Read `.storybook/main.ts` (or `main.js`) to find the `stories` glob patterns.
  Your story files must match those patterns to be picked up by Storybook.

### Step 2: Configure `.storybook/preview.ts` with decorators

Add decorators that wrap every story with the providers your components need.
Without this, most non-trivial components will crash.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` in `.storybook/preview.ts`):
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview';

export const config = definePreview({
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
});
```

Otherwise:
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

const preview = {
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
};
export default preview;
```

Common decorators to add:
- **Theme providers** (e.g. ThemeProvider, MUI ThemeProvider, styled-components, Tailwind)
- **Router** (e.g. MemoryRouter, BrowserRouter mock)
- **State stores** (e.g. Redux Provider, Zustand, Jotai)
- **i18n** (e.g. IntlProvider, I18nextProvider)
- **Global CSS** — import global stylesheets at the top of preview.ts

### Step 3: Write stories for 9 components

Pick 9 real components from the codebase, 3 of each complexity level.
Use the title prefix `AI Generated/<Complexity>/<ComponentName>` so they are grouped
together in the Storybook sidebar.

**Simple (3 components)** — Presentational with few props, no internal state.
Examples: Button, Badge, Avatar, Icon, Label, Chip.
Title format: `AI Generated/Simple/<ComponentName>`

**Medium (3 components)** — Multiple visual variants or composed from simpler components.
Examples: Card, Alert, Input, Select, Tooltip, Tabs.
Title format: `AI Generated/Medium/<ComponentName>`

**Complex (3 components)** — Internal state, side effects, or deep composition.
Examples: Modal, DataTable, Form, Dropdown, Accordion, Sidebar.
Title format: `AI Generated/Complex/<ComponentName>`

For each component, create a `<ComponentName>.stories.ts` file next to the component.
Each file must have at least 2 story exports covering the component's main states.
Make sure the file location and naming matches the `stories` patterns in `.storybook/main.ts`.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` / `config.meta` patterns):

Story format (CSF Factory — this project uses CSF factories):
```ts
import { config } from '#.storybook/preview';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = config.meta({
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
});

export const Default = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
});

export const Disabled = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
});
```

Otherwise:

Story format (CSF):
```ts
import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = {
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
} satisfies Meta<typeof Button>;

export default meta;
type Story = StoryObj<typeof meta>;

export const Default: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
};

export const Disabled: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
};
```

Rules:
- Every named export is a story. Use `args` to set props.
- Provide all required props via `args` — check the component's types.
- If a component needs per-story decorators (beyond the global ones), add them in the meta.
- Do NOT use `any` types. Use the component's prop types for type safety.

Reference: https://storybook.js.org/docs/latest/writing-stories

### Step 4: Verify each story with Vitest

After writing each story file, immediately verify it:

```bash
npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>
```

**Self-healing loop — repeat for every story file:**
1. Write/update the story file
2. Run `npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>`
3. If it fails: read the error output carefully
   - Missing provider → add a decorator in `.storybook/preview.ts` or in the story meta
   - Missing prop → add the required prop to `args`
   - Import error → fix the import path
   - CSS/asset error → add static dirs or import the stylesheet
4. Fix the issue and go back to step 2
5. Once the test passes, move to the next component

After all 9 story files pass individually, run the full suite:
```bash
npx vitest --project storybook
```

### Checklist

- [ ] Analyzed codebase for providers, global styles, and path aliases
- [ ] Read story patterns from `.storybook/main.ts`
- [ ] Configured `.storybook/preview.ts` with necessary decorators
- [ ] Simple component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Full Vitest suite passes: `npx vitest --project storybook`
- [ ] Run `npx storybook doctor` to check for common issues (version mismatches, duplicated deps, etc.)

1→Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or attempt to do them out of order.
     2→
     3→Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by analyzing the codebase,
     4→configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing example stories for 9 components.
     5→
     6→Work through these steps in order. After each story file, run Vitest to verify it renders.
     7→If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it passes before moving on.
     8→
     9→### Step 1: Analyze the codebase
    10→
    11→Before writing any stories, understand what the components need to render:
    12→
    13→- Scan the project for context providers, theme systems, routers, stores, and i18n setups.
    14→  Look at the app's entry point (e.g. `App.tsx`, `main.tsx`, `layout.tsx`) to see what
    15→  providers wrap the component tree.
    16→- Identify global CSS or style imports required for components to look correct.
    17→- Note any path aliases configured in tsconfig or bundler config.
    18→- Read `.storybook/main.ts` (or `main.js`) to find the `stories` glob patterns.
    19→  Your story files must match those patterns to be picked up by Storybook.
    20→
    21→### Step 2: Configure `.storybook/preview.ts` with decorators
    22→
    23→Add decorators that wrap every story with the providers your components need.
    24→Without this, most non-trivial components will crash.
    25→
    26→If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` in `.storybook/preview.ts`):
    27→```ts
    28→// .storybook/preview.ts
    29→import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles
    30→
    31→import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview';
    32→
    33→export const config = definePreview({
    34→  decorators: [
    35→    (Story) => (
    36→      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
    37→        <MemoryRouter>
    38→          <Story />
    39→        </MemoryRouter>
    40→      </ThemeProvider>
    41→    ),
    42→  ],
    43→});
    44→```
    45→
    46→Otherwise:
    47→```ts
    48→// .storybook/preview.ts
    49→import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles
    50→
    51→const preview = {
    52→  decorators: [
    53→    (Story) => (
    54→      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
    55→        <MemoryRouter>
    56→          <Story />
    57→        </MemoryRouter>
    58→      </ThemeProvider>
    59→    ),
    60→  ],
    61→};
    62→export default preview;
    63→```
    64→
    65→Common decorators to add:
    66→- **Theme providers** (e.g. ThemeProvider, MUI ThemeProvider, styled-components, Tailwind)
    67→- **Router** (e.g. MemoryRouter, BrowserRouter mock)
    68→- **State stores** (e.g. Redux Provider, Zustand, Jotai)
    69→- **i18n** (e.g. IntlProvider, I18nextProvider)
    70→- **Global CSS** — import global stylesheets at the top of preview.ts
    71→
    72→### Step 3: Write stories for 9 components
    73→
    74→Pick 9 real components from the codebase, 3 of each complexity level.
    75→Use the title prefix `AI Generated/<Complexity>/<ComponentName>` so they are grouped
    76→together in the Storybook sidebar.
    77→
    78→**Simple (3 components)** — Presentational with few props, no internal state.
    79→Examples: Button, Badge, Avatar, Icon, Label, Chip.
    80→Title format: `AI Generated/Simple/<ComponentName>`
    81→
    82→**Medium (3 components)** — Multiple visual variants or composed from simpler components.
    83→Examples: Card, Alert, Input, Select, Tooltip, Tabs.
    84→Title format: `AI Generated/Medium/<ComponentName>`
    85→
    86→**Complex (3 components)** — Internal state, side effects, or deep composition.
    87→Examples: Modal, DataTable, Form, Dropdown, Accordion, Sidebar.
    88→Title format: `AI Generated/Complex/<ComponentName>`
    89→
    90→For each component, create a `<ComponentName>.stories.ts` file next to the component.
    91→Each file must have at least 2 story exports covering the component's main states.
    92→Make sure the file location and naming matches the `stories` patterns in `.storybook/main.ts`.
    93→
    94→If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` / `config.meta` patterns):
    95→
    96→Story format (CSF Factory — this project uses CSF factories):
    97→```ts
    98→import { config } from '#.storybook/preview';
    99→import { Button } from './Button';
   100→
   101→const meta = config.meta({
   102→  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
   103→  component: Button,
   104→});
   105→
   106→export const Default = meta.story({
   107→  args: {
   108→    label: 'Click me',
   109→  },
   110→});
   111→
   112→export const Disabled = meta.story({
   113→  args: {
   114→    label: 'Disabled',
   115→    disabled: true,
   116→  },
   117→});
   118→```
   119→
   120→Otherwise:
   121→
   122→Story format (CSF):
   123→```ts
   124→import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react';
   125→import { Button } from './Button';
   126→
   127→const meta = {
   128→  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
   129→  component: Button,
   130→} satisfies Meta<typeof Button>;
   131→
   132→export default meta;
   133→type Story = StoryObj<typeof meta>;
   134→
   135→export const Default: Story = {
   136→  args: {
   137→    label: 'Click me',
   138→  },
   139→};
   140→
   141→export const Disabled: Story = {
   142→  args: {
   143→    label: 'Disabled',
   144→    disabled: true,
   145→  },
   146→};
   147→```
   148→
   149→Rules:
   150→- Every named export is a story. Use `args` to set props.
   151→- Provide all required props via `args` — check the component's types.
   152→- If a component needs per-story decorators (beyond the global ones), add them in the meta.
   153→- Do NOT use `any` types. Use the component's prop types for type safety.
   154→
   155→Reference: https://storybook.js.org/docs/latest/writing-stories
   156→
   157→### Step 4: Verify each story with Vitest
   158→
   159→After writing each story file, immediately verify it:
   160→
   161→```bash
   162→npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>
   163→```
   164→
   165→**Self-healing loop — repeat for every story file:**

## Project Analysis Report: Edgy

### 1. Framework
**React 18.3.1** with React Router DOM 7.7.0 for client-side routing.

### 2. App Entry Point
- **Main entry:** `/src/index.jsx` - Creates the React root and renders `<App />`
- **App component:** `/src/App.jsx` - Wraps the entire application with `<BrowserRouter>` from react-router-dom and defines all routes

The App.jsx includes:
- BrowserRouter for navigation
- Route definitions for all pages (Landing, Pricing, Contact, About, Blog, FAQ, Jobs, SignIn, SignUp, Profile, Terms, Privacy, Refunds, NotFound)
- User state management (login status, user data via sessionStorage)
- Airtable database integration for user authentication
- Navigation and Footer components wrapping the routes

### 3. Context Providers, Theme Systems, Routers, Stores, i18n
- **Router:** BrowserRouter from react-router-dom (wrapped in App.jsx at the top level)
- **Theme/Styling:** Tailwind CSS with custom CSS variables for colors:
  - `--primary-font-color: rgb(147, 157, 250)`
  - `--primary-font-color-hover: rgb(82, 98, 245)`
- **State Management:** Basic React hooks (useState, useEffect) + sessionStorage for persistent user data
- **No i18n setup** - not a multi-language app
- **No Redux/Zustand/Jotai** - uses local component state and sessionStorage
- **No context providers** - the router is the main structural provider

### 4. Global CSS/Style Imports
- **Main stylesheet:** `/src/core-ui/index.css`
  - Imports Tailwind CSS with `@import "tailwindcss"`
  - Defines CSS variables for colors
  - Base font and code styling
- **Tailwind Config:** `/tailwind.config.js` - minimal config with content paths
- **Imported in:** `/src/index.jsx` via `./core-ui/index.css`

### 5. Path Aliases
**No path aliases configured** - the project uses standard relative imports throughout.

### 6. Storybook Configuration Files

**.storybook/main.ts:**
```typescript
stories: [
  '../src/**/*.mdx',
  '../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|mjs|ts|tsx)',
  './eval-support/*.mdx',
],
addons: [
  '@chromatic-com/storybook',
  '@storybook/addon-vitest',
  '@storybook/addon-a11y',

  '@storybook/addon-onboarding',
],
framework: '@storybook/react-vite',
```

**.storybook/preview.tsx:**
Uses standard CSF format (NOT CSF Factory). Current setup only has:
```typescript
const preview: Preview = {
  parameters: {
    controls: { matchers: { color, date } },
    a11y: { test: 'todo' },
  },
};
export default preview;
```
**Note:** Missing decorators for BrowserRouter and Tailwind CSS imports.

### 7. Story Files and Glob Patterns
Stories are located in `/src/stories/` directory and match the glob pattern.

**Existing stories (3):**
1. `/src/stories/Button.stories.js` - Example button component with variants (Primary, Secondary, Large, Small)
2. `/src/stories/Header.stories.js` - Example header component (LoggedIn, LoggedOut)
3. `/src/stories/Page.stories.js` - Example page component with interaction test

All use standard CSF format with interaction testing and autodocs tags.

### 8. Components List

**Components in `/src/components/` (7 files):**
- `/src/components/navigation/Navigation.jsx` - Main navigation bar with auth-aware links
- `/src/components/footer/Footer.jsx` - Footer with links and social media
- `/src/components/cta/Cta.jsx` - Call-to-action section
- `/src/components/team/Team.jsx` - Team display component
- `/src/components/JobsForm.jsx` - Jobs application form
- `/src/components/Newsletter.jsx` - Newsletter signup
- `/src/components/NotFound.jsx` - 404 page component

**Routes in `/src/routes/` (38 files across subdirectories):**
- **Landing:** `/src/routes/landing/` - Hero, Partners, Feature, Stats, Pricing, Testimonials, Comparison, Landing
- **Pages:** About, Blog, Contact, FAQ, Jobs, Privacy, Pricing, Refunds, SignIn, SignUp, Terms, UserProfile
- **Forms:** SignUpForm, SignInForm, ContactForm, JobsForm
- **Sub-components:** BlogGrid, CareerCta, AboutOne/Two/Three, etc.

**Stories examples (in `/src/stories/`):**
- `/src/stories/Button.jsx` - Reusable button component
- `/src/stories/Header.jsx` - Reusable header component
- `/src/stories/Page.jsx` - Page template component

### 9. Package.json Dependencies

**Key dependencies:**
- react 18.3.1
- react-dom 18.3.1
- react-router-dom 7.7.0
- @headlessui/react 1.5.0
- @tailwindcss/vite 4.1.11

- moment 2.30.1
- uuid 11.1.0
- cors 2.8.5
- express 4.18.2
- react-google-recaptcha 3.1.0
- dotenv 17.2.0

**Dev dependencies:**
- storybook 0.0.0-pr-34421-sha-0f73e3a9 (pre-release with Vitest integration)
- @storybook/react-vite 0.0.0-pr-34421-sha-0f73e3a9
- @storybook/addon-vitest, addon-a11y, addon-docs, addon-onboarding
- vite 7.0.5

**Vite config** (`vite.config.js`):
- React plugin + Tailwind CSS plugin
- Vitest integration with Storybook addon
- Browser testing via Playwright
- History API fallback for SPA routing
- CSS code splitting enabled

**Scripts:**
- `npm run dev` - Vite dev server
- `npm run build` - Vite build
- `npm run storybook` - Storybook on port 6006
- `npm run build-storybook` - Static Storybook build

The project needs Storybook stories for 9 components organized by complexity level. The preview.tsx file needs to be updated with decorators to wrap stories with BrowserRouter and import global styles (Tailwind CSS). Currently, only 3 example stories exist in the `/src/stories/` directory. The evaluation task requires writing stories for 9 real application components (3 simple, 3 medium, 3 complex) and running them through Vitest.

transcript.prompt:
Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or attempt to do them out of order.

Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by analyzing the codebase,
configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing example stories for 9 components.

Work through these steps in order. After each story file, run Vitest to verify it renders.
If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it passes before moving on.

### Step 1: Analyze the codebase

Before writing any stories, understand what the components need to render:

- Scan the project for context providers, theme systems, routers, stores, and i18n setups.
  Look at the app's entry point (e.g. `App.tsx`, `main.tsx`, `layout.tsx`) to see what
  providers wrap the component tree.
- Identify global CSS or style imports required for components to look correct.
- Note any path aliases configured in tsconfig or bundler config.
- Read `.storybook/main.ts` (or `main.js`) to find the `stories` glob patterns.
  Your story files must match those patterns to be picked up by Storybook.

### Step 2: Configure `.storybook/preview.ts` with decorators

Add decorators that wrap every story with the providers your components need.
Without this, most non-trivial components will crash.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` in `.storybook/preview.ts`):
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview';

export const config = definePreview({
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
});
```

Otherwise:
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

const preview = {
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
};
export default preview;
```

Common decorators to add:
- **Theme providers** (e.g. ThemeProvider, MUI ThemeProvider, styled-components, Tailwind)
- **Router** (e.g. MemoryRouter, BrowserRouter mock)
- **State stores** (e.g. Redux Provider, Zustand, Jotai)
- **i18n** (e.g. IntlProvider, I18nextProvider)
- **Global CSS** — import global stylesheets at the top of preview.ts

### Step 3: Write stories for 9 components

Pick 9 real components from the codebase, 3 of each complexity level.
Use the title prefix `AI Generated/<Complexity>/<ComponentName>` so they are grouped
together in the Storybook sidebar.

**Simple (3 components)** — Presentational with few props, no internal state.
Examples: Button, Badge, Avatar, Icon, Label, Chip.
Title format: `AI Generated/Simple/<ComponentName>`

**Medium (3 components)** — Multiple visual variants or composed from simpler components.
Examples: Card, Alert, Input, Select, Tooltip, Tabs.
Title format: `AI Generated/Medium/<ComponentName>`

**Complex (3 components)** — Internal state, side effects, or deep composition.
Examples: Modal, DataTable, Form, Dropdown, Accordion, Sidebar.
Title format: `AI Generated/Complex/<ComponentName>`

For each component, create a `<ComponentName>.stories.ts` file next to the component.
Each file must have at least 2 story exports covering the component's main states.
Make sure the file location and naming matches the `stories` patterns in `.storybook/main.ts`.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` / `config.meta` patterns):

Story format (CSF Factory — this project uses CSF factories):
```ts
import { config } from '#.storybook/preview';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = config.meta({
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
});

export const Default = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
});

export const Disabled = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
});
```

Otherwise:

Story format (CSF):
```ts
import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = {
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
} satisfies Meta<typeof Button>;

export default meta;
type Story = StoryObj<typeof meta>;

export const Default: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
};

export const Disabled: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
};
```

Rules:
- Every named export is a story. Use `args` to set props.
- Provide all required props via `args` — check the component's types.
- If a component needs per-story decorators (beyond the global ones), add them in the meta.
- Do NOT use `any` types. Use the component's prop types for type safety.

Reference: https://storybook.js.org/docs/latest/writing-stories

### Step 4: Verify each story with Vitest

After writing each story file, immediately verify it:

```bash
npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>
```

**Self-healing loop — repeat for every story file:**
1. Write/update the story file
2. Run `npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>`
3. If it fails: read the error output carefully
   - Missing provider → add a decorator in `.storybook/preview.ts` or in the story meta
   - Missing prop → add the required prop to `args`
   - Import error → fix the import path
   - CSS/asset error → add static dirs or import the stylesheet
4. Fix the issue and go back to step 2
5. Once the test passes, move to the next component

After all 9 story files pass individually, run the full suite:
```bash
npx vitest --project storybook
```

### Checklist

- [ ] Analyzed codebase for providers, global styles, and path aliases
- [ ] Read story patterns from `.storybook/main.ts`
- [ ] Configured `.storybook/preview.ts` with necessary decorators
- [ ] Simple component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Full Vitest suite passes: `npx vitest --project storybook`
- [ ] Run `npx storybook doctor` to check for common issues (version mismatches, duplicated deps, etc.)

1→Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or attempt to do them out of order.
     2→
     3→Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by analyzing the codebase,
     4→configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing example stories for 9 components.
     5→
     6→Work through these steps in order. After each story file, run Vitest to verify it renders.
     7→If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it passes before moving on.
     8→
     9→### Step 1: Analyze the codebase
    10→
    11→Before writing any stories, understand what the components need to render:
    12→
    13→- Scan the project for context providers, theme systems, routers, stores, and i18n setups.
    14→  Look at the app's entry point (e.g. `App.tsx`, `main.tsx`, `layout.tsx`) to see what
    15→  providers wrap the component tree.
    16→- Identify global CSS or style imports required for components to look correct.
    17→- Note any path aliases configured in tsconfig or bundler config.
    18→- Read `.storybook/main.ts` (or `main.js`) to find the `stories` glob patterns.
    19→  Your story files must match those patterns to be picked up by Storybook.
    20→
    21→### Step 2: Configure `.storybook/preview.ts` with decorators
    22→
    23→Add decorators that wrap every story with the providers your components need.
    24→Without this, most non-trivial components will crash.
    25→
    26→If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` in `.storybook/preview.ts`):
    27→```ts
    28→// .storybook/preview.ts
    29→import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles
    30→
    31→import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview';
    32→
    33→export const config = definePreview({
    34→  decorators: [
    35→    (Story) => (
    36→      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
    37→        <MemoryRouter>
    38→          <Story />
    39→        </MemoryRouter>
    40→      </ThemeProvider>
    41→    ),
    42→  ],
    43→});
    44→```
    45→
    46→Otherwise:
    47→```ts
    48→// .storybook/preview.ts
    49→import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles
    50→
    51→const preview = {
    52→  decorators: [
    53→    (Story) => (
    54→      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
    55→        <MemoryRouter>
    56→          <Story />
    57→        </MemoryRouter>
    58→      </ThemeProvider>
    59→    ),
    60→  ],
    61→};
    62→export default preview;
    63→```
    64→
    65→Common decorators to add:
    66→- **Theme providers** (e.g. ThemeProvider, MUI ThemeProvider, styled-components, Tailwind)
    67→- **Router** (e.g. MemoryRouter, BrowserRouter mock)
    68→- **State stores** (e.g. Redux Provider, Zustand, Jotai)
    69→- **i18n** (e.g. IntlProvider, I18nextProvider)
    70→- **Global CSS** — import global stylesheets at the top of preview.ts
    71→
    72→### Step 3: Write stories for 9 components
    73→
    74→Pick 9 real components from the codebase, 3 of each complexity level.
    75→Use the title prefix `AI Generated/<Complexity>/<ComponentName>` so they are grouped
    76→together in the Storybook sidebar.
    77→
    78→**Simple (3 components)** — Presentational with few props, no internal state.
    79→Examples: Button, Badge, Avatar, Icon, Label, Chip.
    80→Title format: `AI Generated/Simple/<ComponentName>`
    81→
    82→**Medium (3 components)** — Multiple visual variants or composed from simpler components.
    83→Examples: Card, Alert, Input, Select, Tooltip, Tabs.
    84→Title format: `AI Generated/Medium/<ComponentName>`
    85→
    86→**Complex (3 components)** — Internal state, side effects, or deep composition.
    87→Examples: Modal, DataTable, Form, Dropdown, Accordion, Sidebar.
    88→Title format: `AI Generated/Complex/<ComponentName>`
    89→
    90→For each component, create a `<ComponentName>.stories.ts` file next to the component.
    91→Each file must have at least 2 story exports covering the component's main states.
    92→Make sure the file location and naming matches the `stories` patterns in `.storybook/main.ts`.
    93→
    94→If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` / `config.meta` patterns):
    95→
    96→Story format (CSF Factory — this project uses CSF factories):
    97→```ts
    98→import { config } from '#.storybook/preview';
    99→import { Button } from './Button';
   100→
   101→const meta = config.meta({
   102→  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
   103→  component: Button,
   104→});
   105→
   106→export const Default = meta.story({
   107→  args: {
   108→    label: 'Click me',
   109→  },
   110→});
   111→
   112→export const Disabled = meta.story({
   113→  args: {
   114→    label: 'Disabled',
   115→    disabled: true,
   116→  },
   117→});
   118→```
   119→
   120→Otherwise:
   121→
   122→Story format (CSF):
   123→```ts
   124→import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react';
   125→import { Button } from './Button';
   126→
   127→const meta = {
   128→  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
   129→  component: Button,
   130→} satisfies Meta<typeof Button>;
   131→
   132→export default meta;
   133→type Story = StoryObj<typeof meta>;
   134→
   135→export const Default: Story = {
   136→  args: {
   137→    label: 'Click me',
   138→  },
   139→};
   140→
   141→export const Disabled: Story = {
   142→  args: {
   143→    label: 'Disabled',
   144→    disabled: true,
   145→  },
   146→};
   147→```
   148→
   149→Rules:
   150→- Every named export is a story. Use `args` to set props.
   151→- Provide all required props via `args` — check the component's types.
   152→- If a component needs per-story decorators (beyond the global ones), add them in the meta.
   153→- Do NOT use `any` types. Use the component's prop types for type safety.
   154→
   155→Reference: https://storybook.js.org/docs/latest/writing-stories
   156→
   157→### Step 4: Verify each story with Vitest
   158→
   159→After writing each story file, immediately verify it:
   160→
   161→```bash
   162→npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>
   163→```
   164→
   165→**Self-healing loop — repeat for every story file:**
   166→1. Write/update the story file
   167→2. Run `npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>`
   168→3. If it fails: read the error output carefully
   169→   - Missing provider → add a decorator in `.storybook/preview.ts` or in the story meta
   170→   - Missing prop → add the required prop to `args`
   171→   - Import error → fix the import path
   172→   - CSS/asset error → add static dirs or import the stylesheet
   173→4. Fix the issue and go back to step 2
   174→5. Once the test passes, move to the next component
   175→
   176→After all 9 story files pass individually, run the full suite:
   177→```bash
   178→npx vitest --project storybook
   179→```

## Project Analysis Report: Edgy

### 1. Framework
**React 18.3.1** with React Router DOM 7.7.0 for client-side routing.

### 2. App Entry Point
- **Main entry:** `/src/index.jsx` - Creates the React root and renders `<App />`
- **App component:** `/src/App.jsx` - Wraps the entire application with `<BrowserRouter>` from react-router-dom and defines all routes

The App.jsx includes:
- BrowserRouter for navigation
- Route definitions for all pages (Landing, Pricing, Contact, About, Blog, FAQ, Jobs, SignIn, SignUp, Profile, Terms, Privacy, Refunds, NotFound)
- User state management (login status, user data via sessionStorage)
- Airtable database integration for user authentication
- Navigation and Footer components wrapping the routes

### 3. Context Providers, Theme Systems, Routers, Stores, i18n
- **Router:** BrowserRouter from react-router-dom (wrapped in App.jsx at the top level)
- **Theme/Styling:** Tailwind CSS with custom CSS variables for colors:
  - `--primary-font-color: rgb(147, 157, 250)`
  - `--primary-font-color-hover: rgb(82, 98, 245)`
- **State Management:** Basic React hooks (useState, useEffect) + sessionStorage for persistent user data
- **No i18n setup** - not a multi-language app
- **No Redux/Zustand/Jotai** - uses local component state and sessionStorage
- **No context providers** - the router is the main structural provider

### 4. Global CSS/Style Imports
- **Main stylesheet:** `/src/core-ui/index.css`
  - Imports Tailwind CSS with `@import "tailwindcss"`
  - Defines CSS variables for colors
  - Base font and code styling
- **Tailwind Config:** `/tailwind.config.js` - minimal config with content paths
- **Imported in:** `/src/index.jsx` via `./core-ui/index.css`

### 5. Path Aliases
**No path aliases configured** - the project uses standard relative imports throughout.

### 6. Storybook Configuration Files

**.storybook/main.ts:**
```typescript
stories: [
  '../src/**/*.mdx',
  '../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|mjs|ts|tsx)',
  './eval-support/*.mdx',
],
addons: [
  '@chromatic-com/storybook',
  '@storybook/addon-vitest',
  '@storybook/addon-a11y',
  '@storybook/addon-docs',
  '@storybook/addon-onboarding',
],
framework: '@storybook/react-vite',
```

**.storybook/preview.tsx:**
Uses standard CSF format (NOT CSF Factory). Current setup only has:
```typescript
const preview: Preview = {
  parameters: {
    controls: { matchers: { color, date } },
    a11y: { test: 'todo' },
  },
};
export default preview;
```
**Note:** Missing decorators for BrowserRouter and Tailwind CSS imports.

### 7. Story Files and Glob Patterns
Stories are located in `/src/stories/` directory and match the glob pattern.

**Existing stories (3):**
1. `/src/stories/Button.stories.js` - Example button component with variants (Primary, Secondary, Large, Small)
2. `/src/stories/Header.stories.js` - Example header component (LoggedIn, LoggedOut)
3. `/src/stories/Page.stories.js` - Example page component with interaction test

All use standard CSF format with interaction testing and autodocs tags.

### 8. Components List

**Components in `/src/components/` (7 files):**
- `/src/components/navigation/Navigation.jsx` - Main navigation bar with auth-aware links
- `/src/components/footer/Footer.jsx` - Footer with links and social media
- `/src/components/cta/Cta.jsx` - Call-to-action section
- `/src/components/team/Team.jsx` - Team display component
- `/src/components/JobsForm.jsx` - Jobs application form
- `/src/components/Newsletter.jsx` - Newsletter signup
- `/src/components/NotFound.jsx` - 404 page component

**Routes in `/src/routes/` (38 files across subdirectories):**
- **Landing:** `/src/routes/landing/` - Hero, Partners, Feature, Stats, Pricing, Testimonials, Comparison, Landing
- **Pages:** About, Blog, Contact, FAQ, Jobs, Privacy, Pricing, Refunds, SignIn, SignUp, Terms, UserProfile
- **Forms:** SignUpForm, SignInForm, ContactForm, JobsForm
- **Sub-components:** BlogGrid, CareerCta, AboutOne/Two/Three, etc.

**Stories examples (in `/src/stories/`):**
- `/src/stories/Button.jsx` - Reusable button component
- `/src/stories/Header.jsx` - Reusable header component
- `/src/stories/Page.jsx` - Page template component

### 9. Package.json Dependencies

**Key dependencies:**
- react 18.3.1
- react-dom 18.3.1
- react-router-dom 7.7.0
- @headlessui/react 1.5.0
- @tailwindcss/vite 4.1.11
- airtable 0.12.2
- moment 2.30.1
- uuid 11.1.0
- cors 2.8.5
- express 4.18.2
- react-google-recaptcha 3.1.0
- dotenv 17.2.0
- node-fetch 3.3.2

**Dev dependencies:**
- storybook 0.0.0-pr-34421-sha-0f73e3a9 (pre-release with Vitest integration)
- @storybook/react-vite 0.0.0-pr-34421-sha-0f73e3a9
- @storybook/addon-vitest, addon-a11y, addon-docs, addon-onboarding
- vite 7.0.5
- vitest 4.1.2
- playwright 1.58.2
- @vitest/browser-playwright 4.1.2
- @chromatic-com/storybook 5.1.1

### 10. Build and Dev Configuration

**Vite config** (`vite.config.js`):
- React plugin + Tailwind CSS plugin
- Vitest integration with Storybook addon
- Browser testing via Playwright
- History API fallback for SPA routing
- CSS code splitting enabled

**Scripts:**
- `npm run dev` - Vite dev server
- `npm run build` - Vite build
- `npm run storybook` - Storybook on port 6006
- `npm run build-storybook` - Static Storybook build

The project needs Storybook stories for 9 components organized by complexity level. The preview.tsx file needs to be updated with decorators to wrap stories with BrowserRouter and import global styles (Tailwind CSS). Currently, only 3 example stories exist in the `/src/stories/` directory. The evaluation task requires writing stories for 9 real application components (3 simple, 3 medium, 3 complex) and running them through Vitest.

Perfect! I've successfully read all the requested files. Here's a complete report of their contents:

## File Contents Report

### 1. Stats.jsx
**Location:** `/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T18-39-20Z-9dd8ead6/project/src/routes/landing/Stats.jsx`

A React component displaying company statistics with a background image. It imports an Instagram preview image and company stats data, then renders them in a grid layout with Tailwind styling. The component uses a dark gray background (bg-gray-900) and displays metrics in a 1-2 column layout depending on screen size.

### 2. Feature.jsx
**Location:** `/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T18-39-20Z-9dd8ead6/project/src/routes/landing/Feature.jsx`

A features showcase component that maps over a features data array imported from `../../data/features`. It displays features in a 3-column responsive grid with feature icons, names, and descriptions. Each feature is contained in a card with rounded corners and shadow effects. The component has a dark background with white headings and gray body text.

### 3. Hero.jsx
**Location:** `/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T18-39-20Z-9dd8ead6/project/src/routes/landing/Hero.jsx`

A hero section component with form handling and validation. Features include:
- Email input field with custom validation using regex
- Form state management with useState hooks
- Loading spinner while submitting
- Success message display after valid submission
- Email validation error messages
- Instagram cover image display on the right side
- Links to terms of service

### 4. Faq.jsx
**Location:** `/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T18-39-20Z-9dd8ead6/project/src/routes/faq/Faq.jsx`

A simple FAQ page component that imports and renders a FaqList component along with a Cta (Call-to-Action) component. Dark background styling with centered heading.

### 5. ContactForm.jsx
**Location:** `/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T18-39-20Z-9dd8ead6/project/src/routes/contact/ContactForm.jsx`

A comprehensive contact form with:
- Multiple form fields: firstName, lastName, email, company, phone, textarea
- Form validation for all fields with minimum length checks
- ReCAPTCHA integration for security
- Loading spinner during submission
- Success message after valid submission
- Error messages displayed below each field
- Environment variable configuration for CAPTCHA keys

### 6. BlogGrid.jsx
**Location:** `/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T18-39-20Z-9dd8ead6/project/src/routes/blog/BlogGrid.jsx`

A blog post grid component that:
- Maps over blogPostsData array
- Displays posts in a responsive 3-column grid
- Shows post image, title, description, author info, and reading time
- Uses react-router-dom Link for navigation with scroll reset
- Dark background styling with hover effects on post titles

### 7. SignInForm.jsx
**Location:** `/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T18-39-20Z-9dd8ead6/project/src/routes/sign-in/SignInForm.jsx`

A sign-in form component with:
- Email and password input fields
- Form validation (email regex, minimum 8-character password)
- ReCAPTCHA verification
- Async database retrieval via retrieveDatabase prop
- Loading state with spinner and message
- Error handling for invalid credentials
- Navigation to profile page on successful login

### 8. team.js
**Location:** `/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T18-39-20Z-9dd8ead6/project/src/data/team.js`

A data export file containing a team array with 6 team members. Each team member object includes:
- name (string)
- role (string)
- imageUrl (Unsplash photo URL)
- twitterUrl
- linkedinUrl

Team members: Whitney Francis (Manager), Charly Stuart (Team Leader), Fox Lister (Growth Specialist), Steven Francis (Head of Marketing), David Wisley (Sales Manager), and Colt Denson (Customer Support).
